title: Unsafe deserialization via BinaryFormatter
summary: BinaryFormatter.Deserialize, SoapFormatter.Deserialize, NetDataContractSerializer.Deserialize, or JsonConvert.DeserializeObject with TypeNameHandling is invoked on untrusted input. These APIs instantiate arbitrary types, enabling reliable remote code execution.
why_it_matters: Unsafe deserialization sinks in .NET are weaponized routinely via public gadget chains (ysoserial.net ships dozens). A single untrusted input point reaching BinaryFormatter.Deserialize is effectively a remote code execution primitive — attackers can construct payloads that spawn shells, exfiltrate data, or pivot across the network. Microsoft marked BinaryFormatter obsolete as of .NET 5 for this exact reason.
how_to_fix:
Replace legacy serializers with safe, strongly-typed alternatives:

1. Prefer System.Text.Json.JsonSerializer for all new code. It is type-safe, fast, and does NOT instantiate arbitrary types.
2. If you need XML, use DataContractSerializer or XmlSerializer with a fixed known type (pass typeof(MyDto) to the constructor).
3. If you must use Newtonsoft.Json, ensure TypeNameHandling = TypeNameHandling.None (the default). Never set it to All, Auto, or Objects for untrusted input.
4. Delete all uses of BinaryFormatter, SoapFormatter, NetDataContractSerializer, LosFormatter, and ObjectStateFormatter — these APIs cannot be used safely on untrusted data.
5. If you're maintaining legacy code that cannot be rewritten, isolate deserialization in a sandboxed process with restricted permissions and network egress blocked.
developer_notes: If you see BinaryFormatter being used to persist objects to disk or cache them in Redis, the fix usually involves defining a DTO class, serializing with System.Text.Json, and storing the JSON string. This is safer and more portable across .NET versions. The tradeoff is that System.Text.Json cannot round-trip arbitrary object graphs the way BinaryFormatter can — this is a feature, not a limitation.
verification_checklist: No references to BinaryFormatter, SoapFormatter, NetDataContractSerializer, LosFormatter, or ObjectStateFormatter anywhere in the codebase
JsonConvert.DeserializeObject calls do not enable TypeNameHandling.All / Auto / Objects
System.Text.Json.JsonSerializer is used with concrete DTO types, not object or dynamic
XmlSerializer and DataContractSerializer are constructed with a fixed known type, not a type loaded from user input
